description.en: Deploy FacebookAI/roberta-large for fill-mask inference across PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and ONNX — compare latency, memory, and prediction quality while mastering the training improvements that made RoBERTa outperform BERT on every benchmark.

story_variants.en:
## RoBERTa-Large Across Every Framework

Vaathiyaar here! `FacebookAI/roberta-large` is a rare breed — a 355M-parameter encoder model that ships with first-class support for **PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and ONNX**. That means you can train on GPU with PyTorch, deploy to TPU with JAX, serve via TF Serving, or optimize for CPU with ONNX — all from the same checkpoint. Today we'll run fill-mask inference across all four frameworks and understand what makes RoBERTa's masked predictions superior to BERT's.

### Why RoBERTa Predicts Better Than BERT

| Training Decision | BERT | RoBERTa | Impact on Fill-Mask |
|---|---|---|---|
| Masking | Static (same masks) | Dynamic (new masks each epoch) | Richer prediction diversity |
| Training data | 16 GB | 160 GB | Broader world knowledge |
| Tokenizer | WordPiece 30K | BPE 50K | Fewer `<unk>` tokens, finer predictions |
| NSP objective | Yes | Removed | Cleaner single-sentence representations |
| Mask token | `[MASK]` | `<mask>` | Different syntax, same concept |

### PyTorch: The Default Path

```python
from transformers import pipeline

unmasker = pipeline("fill-mask", model="FacebookAI/roberta-large")
results = unmasker("Deep learning has <mask> the field of AI.")

for r in results[:3]:
    print(f"{r['score']:.4f} => {r['token_str']}")
# 0.3812 =>  transformed
# 0.1547 =>  revolutionized
# 0.0923 =>  changed
```

PyTorch is the default backend. The model (~1.4GB) downloads once and caches. RoBERTa's 24 transformer layers and 1024 hidden dimensions produce nuanced, semantically rich predictions.

### TensorFlow: For TF Serving Deployments

```python
from transformers import TFAutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer
import tensorflow as tf

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")
model = TFAutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")

text = "The best way to learn programming is by <mask>."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="tf")
outputs = model(**inputs)

mask_idx = tf.where(inputs["input_ids"] == tokenizer.mask_token_id)[0][1]
logits = outputs.logits[0, mask_idx]
top_5 = tf.math.top_k(logits, k=5)

for score, idx in zip(top_5.values.numpy(), top_5.indices.numpy()):
    print(f"{score:.2f} => {tokenizer.decode([idx]).strip()}")
```

Same weights, same predictions — just TensorFlow tensors instead of PyTorch. Ideal when your production stack uses TF Serving or TFLite.

### JAX/Flax: For TPU Training

```python
from transformers import FlaxAutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer
import jax.numpy as jnp

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")
model = FlaxAutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")

text = "Scientists recently <mask> a new species."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="jax")
outputs = model(**inputs)

mask_idx = jnp.where(inputs["input_ids"] == tokenizer.mask_token_id)[1][0]
top_5_idx = jnp.argsort(outputs.logits[0, mask_idx])[-5:][::-1]

for idx in top_5_idx:
    print(tokenizer.decode([int(idx)]).strip())
```

JAX's XLA compilation makes RoBERTa fly on TPUs. Flax models use the same pre-trained weights, so you can train on TPU and export back to PyTorch for deployment.

### ONNX: Fastest CPU Inference

```python
from optimum.onnxruntime import ORTModelForMaskedLM
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")
model = ORTModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained(
    "FacebookAI/roberta-large", export=True
)

results = model(**tokenizer("AI will <mask> every industry.",
                            return_tensors="pt"))
# ONNX: 2-3x faster on CPU than PyTorch
```

ONNX Runtime with graph optimizations and optional INT8 quantization delivers the fastest CPU inference — critical for edge deployment and cost-sensitive production.

### Framework Selection Guide

| Framework | Best For | Latency (relative) | Memory |
|---|---|---|---|
| PyTorch | Training, research, GPU serving | 1.0x (baseline) | ~1.4 GB |
| TensorFlow | TF Serving, TFLite mobile | ~1.1x | ~1.5 GB |
| JAX/Flax | TPU training, XLA compilation | ~0.8x (TPU) | ~1.4 GB |
| ONNX | CPU production, edge, quantized | ~0.4x (CPU) | ~0.7 GB (INT8) |

> **Key Insight:** RoBERTa-large's multi-framework support means your model choice and your deployment choice are independent decisions. Train in PyTorch for ecosystem richness, fine-tune on TPU via JAX for speed, serve via TF Serving for scale, or export to ONNX for cost-efficient CPU inference. The 355M parameters and 50K BPE vocabulary produce identical high-quality fill-mask predictions across all four frameworks — choose based on your infrastructure, not your model.

animation_sequence.content: RoBERTa-large (355M params) ships with first-class support for PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and ONNX. Train on GPU, fine-tune on TPU, serve via TF Serving, or deploy to CPU with ONNX — all from the same checkpoint. Its dynamic masking and 160GB training data produce superior fill-mask predictions across every framework.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import pipeline

unmasker = pipeline("fill-mask", model="FacebookAI/roberta-large")

results = unmasker("Deep learning has <mask> the field of AI.")

for r in results[:3]:
    print(f"{r['score']:.4f} => {r['token_str']}")
# 0.3812 =>  transformed
# 0.1547 =>  revolutionized

steps.explanation: Import the pipeline abstraction — the simplest way to run any Hugging Face model for inference without managing tokenizers or models separately.
Create a fill-mask pipeline loading FacebookAI/roberta-large. The 355M parameter model (~1.4GB) downloads once and caches in ~/.cache/huggingface.
Pass a sentence with <mask> — RoBERTa's mask token. Unlike BERT which uses [MASK], RoBERTa follows GPT-2 style angle-bracket special tokens.
Slice to top 3 predictions. Each result dict contains 'score' (probability), 'token_str' (predicted word), 'token' (vocab ID), and 'sequence' (filled sentence).
RoBERTa predicts 'transformed' with 38% confidence — trained on 160GB of text, it has rich contextual understanding of how AI relates to field transformation.
'revolutionized' as the second prediction shows semantic diversity. RoBERTa's 50K BPE vocabulary covers more words than BERT's 30K WordPiece.

animation_sequence.code:
from transformers import TFAutoModelForMaskedLM, AutoTokenizer
import tensorflow as tf

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")
model = TFAutoModelForMaskedLM.from_pretrained("FacebookAI/roberta-large")

text = "The best way to learn programming is by <mask>."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors="tf")
outputs = model(**inputs)

mask_idx = tf.where(inputs["input_ids"] == tokenizer.mask_token_id)[0][1]
logits = outputs.logits[0, mask_idx]
top_5 = tf.math.top_k(logits, k=5)

for score, idx in zip(top_5.values.numpy(), top_5.indices.numpy()):
    print(f"{score:.2f} => {tokenizer.decode([idx]).strip()}")

steps.explanation: Import TFAutoModelForMaskedLM — the TensorFlow version of the masked language model class. AutoTokenizer works across all frameworks.
Load the tokenizer — framework-agnostic. The same BPE tokenizer with 50,265 tokens works for PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and ONNX.
Load the TensorFlow model. Hugging Face auto-converts the PyTorch weights to TF format on first load, then caches the TF checkpoint.
Tokenize with return_tensors='tf' to get TensorFlow tensors. The tokenizer adds <s> (start) and </s> (end) tokens automatically.
Forward pass through 24 TF layers. outputs.logits has shape (batch, seq_len, 50265) — one score per vocabulary token per position.
Find the <mask> position by searching for the mask_token_id in input_ids. tf.where returns the coordinates of matching elements.
Extract logits at the mask position — a 50,265-dim vector of raw scores. Higher scores mean higher predicted probability.
tf.math.top_k efficiently finds the 5 highest-scoring tokens without sorting the full 50K vocabulary.
Decode token IDs back to strings. The predictions are identical to PyTorch — same weights produce same results regardless of framework.

items.left: Best for training and GPU inference
items.right: Best for CPU production and edge deployment
Static optimized graph with operator fusion
2-3x faster CPU inference via ONNX Runtime

animation_sequence.message: You can now deploy RoBERTa-large fill-mask across PyTorch, TensorFlow, JAX, and ONNX — choose the right framework for your infrastructure!

instruction.en: Build a `MultiFrameworkFillMask` class that simulates running fill-mask inference across different frameworks. It takes a vocabulary with word-to-context mappings and a set of framework configurations (each framework has a speed_factor and a precision affecting score rounding). Implement `predict(sentence, framework)` that finds the `<mask>` position, scores vocabulary words by context overlap, adjusts scores by the framework's speed_factor, and rounds to the framework's precision. Then implement `compare_frameworks(sentence)` that returns predictions from all frameworks sorted by framework name.

en: In predict(), split the sentence into words, find '<mask>', then for each vocab word count how many of its context words appear in the sentence words (excluding '<mask>')
Multiply the raw count by the framework's speed_factor and round using round(score, precision)
In compare_frameworks(), call predict() for each framework key and return a dict sorted by framework name

practice_challenges.test_code:
v = {"cat": ["pet", "fur"], "dog": ["pet", "bark"], "fish": ["water"]}
f = {"fast": {"speed_factor": 2.0, "precision": 1}, "slow": {"speed_factor": 0.5, "precision": 3}}
m = MultiFrameworkFillMask(v, f)
p_fast = m.predict("my pet has <mask> and fur", "fast")
assert p_fast[0]["token"] == "cat"
assert p_fast[0]["score"] == 4.0
p_slow = m.predict("my pet has <mask> and fur", "slow")
assert p_slow[0]["token"] == "cat"
assert p_slow[0]["score"] == 1.0
c = m.compare_frameworks("my pet has <mask> and fur")
assert "fast" in c and "slow" in c
assert len(c) == 2
assert c["fast"][0]["score"] > c["slow"][0]["score"]

instruction.en: Build a `BPETokenizer` class that simulates RoBERTa's Byte-Pair Encoding tokenization. Given a list of merge rules (pairs of strings to combine), implement `tokenize(text)` that starts by splitting each word into individual characters, then iteratively applies merges in priority order. Prefix subword pieces after the first in each word with 'G' (representing RoBERTa's byte-level continuation). Wrap output with `<s>` and `</s>` tokens.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class BPETokenizer:
    def __init__(self, merges: list[tuple[str, str]]):
        # merges: ordered list of (left, right) merge rules
        # e.g., [("l", "e"), ("le", "a"), ("lea", "rn"), ...]
        self.merges = merges

    def _apply_merges(self, tokens: list[str]) -> list[str]:
        # Apply each merge rule in order:
        # Scan tokens for adjacent (left, right) pair,
        # merge them into one token, repeat for each rule
        pass

    def tokenize(self, text: str) -> list[str]:
        # 1. Lowercase and split into words
        # 2. Split each word into individual characters
        # 3. Apply all merge rules via _apply_merges
        # 4. First token of each word stays as-is,
        #    subsequent tokens get 'G' prefix
        # 5. Wrap with <s> and </s>
        pass

# Test it:
merges = [
    ("l", "e"),    # l + e -> le
    ("a", "r"),    # a + r -> ar
    ("le", "ar"),   # le + ar -> lear
    ("n", "i"),    # n + i -> ni
    ("ni", "n"),   # ni + n -> nin
    ("lear", "nin"),# lear + nin -> learnin
    ("learnin", "g"),# learnin + g -> learning
    ("i", "s"),    # i + s -> is
    ("f", "u"),    # f + u -> fu
    ("fu", "n"),   # fu + n -> fun
]

tokenizer = BPETokenizer(merges)
tokens = tokenizer.tokenize("learning is fun")
print(tokens)
print(len(tokens))

en: In _apply_merges(), for each merge rule (left, right), scan the token list for adjacent pairs matching left and right, combine them into left+right, and produce a new shorter list
In tokenize(), split each word into list(word) first, then call _apply_merges on each word's character list independently
After merging, the first resulting token of each word keeps its value; any remaining tokens in the same word get prefixed with 'G'

practice_challenges.test_code:
m = [("a", "b"), ("ab", "c")]
t = BPETokenizer(m)
tokens = t.tokenize("abc")
assert tokens[0] == "<s>"
assert tokens[-1] == "</s>"
assert "abc" in tokens
m2 = [("h", "i")]
t2 = BPETokenizer(m2)
tokens2 = t2.tokenize("hi hey")
assert tokens2[0] == "<s>"
assert tokens2[-1] == "</s>"
assert "hi" in tokens2
# 'hey' splits to ['h','e','y'], merge h+? no 'i' so stays ['h','e','y'] -> 'h', 'Ge', 'Gy'
assert "Ge" in tokens2 or "Gy" in tokens2
